14/ The central policy question isn’t whether decarbonisation should proceed. It’s whether Europe can combine climate policy with industrial policy, energy security and territorially targeted support so that the regions facing the highest adjustment pressures remain integrated into the transition.

13/ Europe’s competitiveness problem is real, but the record doesn’t support the idea that EU climate policy has been a major drag on competitiveness. But the political economy of the transition is shaped less by aggregate effects than concentrated adjustment costs in specific regions and sectors.

12/ Countries that are more heavily exposed to transition risks generally show lower support for climate neutrality and for shifting subsidies away from fossil fuels. Support for decarbonisation tends to be weakest where the adjustment pressures of the transition are expected to be greatest.
